does: This paper empirically investigates how different fine-tuning methods affect the chain-of-thought reasoning capabilities of large language models. The authors examine multiple fine-tuning approaches including supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF), and quantized low-rank adapters (Q-LoRA), analyzing whether task-specific adaptation improves, maintains, or degrades the models' ability to perform reasoning through intermediate step-by-step explanations. The work provides systematic evaluation and measurement of how these fine-tuning strategies impact both task-specific performance and the transfer of reasoning abilities across different problem domains.
connects: This work builds on established research in instruction-tuning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods that have shown improvements in LLM performance, while responding to recent concerns about catastrophic forgetting and safety degradation during adaptation. It extends prior investigations into chain-of-thought prompting, which demonstrated that explicit reasoning steps improve model outputs across reasoning tasks. The paper addresses a gap identified by recent studies showing that fine-tuning success on target tasks may come at hidden costs—combining threads from reasoning capability research, continual learning literature on catastrophic forgetting, and emerging work on fine-tuning tradeoffs.
enables: This research enables better design of fine-tuning protocols that preserve or enhance reasoning capabilities while achieving task-specific gains. It provides diagnostic insights for practitioners choosing between fine-tuning methods based on their reasoning requirements. The findings could inform development of selective fine-tuning approaches that protect core reasoning abilities, guide hybrid training strategies combining task adaptation with reasoning preservation, and establish benchmarks for measuring reasoning-aware fine-tuning quality. This work supports building more robust adaptation methods and points toward understanding fundamental tradeoffs between specialization and generalization in large language models.
